diagrams.title: Defense-in-Depth Security Layers in Cloud Environment
diagrams.description: A vertical layered diagram illustrating the six concentric security layers that protect cloud resources, showing how threats must penetrate each layer sequentially.
elements: Outer layer (bottom): Physical Security - labeled box showing data center icon, biometric scanners, 24/7 surveillance cameras, mantraps
Second layer: Network Security - firewall icon, VPC boundary, DDoS protection shield, IDS/IPS boxes connected to network lines
Third layer: IAM - user icon with lock, MFA device symbol, RBAC matrix representation, least privilege shield
Fourth layer: Application Security - code brackets icon, WAF shield, vulnerability scanner, input validation flowchart
Fifth layer: Data Security - database cylinder icon, encryption key symbol, DLP shield, data classification labels (Public/Internal/Confidential/Restricted)
Sixth layer (top): Endpoint/Workload Security - server icon, antivirus shield, patch management clock, container security badge
Arrows pointing inward showing threat progression blocked at each layer
Shield icons at each layer intersection representing defense controls
Legend showing that each layer is managed by either provider, customer, or shared responsibility
diagrams.purpose: Students will visualize how defense-in-depth works as a layered approach, understanding that multiple security controls must be bypassed for an attack to succeed, reinforcing the importance of implementing security at every layer.

diagrams.title: Cloud Security Shared Responsibility Model
diagrams.description: A horizontal diagram showing the division of security responsibilities between Cloud Provider and Cloud Customer across infrastructure, platform, and application layers.
elements: Left column labeled 'Cloud Provider' - includes physical infrastructure (data centers, power, cooling), network infrastructure (hypervisor, physical switches), underlying compute/storage services
Middle column labeled 'Shared Responsibility' - includes OS patches, network configuration, identity management, encryption options, application security
Right column labeled 'Cloud Customer' - includes data classification, access management, application code, customer data, user activity monitoring
Top section: Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) - customer has most responsibility (OS, runtime, data, applications)
Middle section: Platform as a Service (PaaS) - shared responsibility (runtime, data, applications)
Bottom section: Software as a Service (SaaS) - provider has most responsibility (application, data, identity, endpoints)
Dotted line separating provider and customer domains with responsibility labels
Color coding: Blue for provider responsibility, Green for shared, Red for customer responsibility
diagrams.purpose: Students will understand which security controls are managed by the provider versus the customer based on the cloud service model, clarifying that security is a shared burden and customers retain responsibility for their data and access management.

diagrams.title: Zero Trust Architecture in Cloud
diagrams.description: A network diagram illustrating Zero Trust principles where every access request is verified regardless of location, showing the continuous verification process.
elements: User/Device at external location connected via untrusted network
Arrow pointing to Identity Provider (IdP) with MFA challenge icon
Verification checkpoint with 'Never Trust, Always Verify' label
Conditional Access Policy engine showing evaluation criteria (device compliance, location, risk score)
Microsegmentation boundaries shown as dotted vertical lines dividing network zones
Workload resources (servers, databases, containers) each with individual access controls
Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) icons at each resource
Continuous monitoring loop with analytics and logging
Deny/Allow decision diamonds at each checkpoint
Session isolation icons between different resource types
diagrams.purpose: Students will learn the core principles of Zero Trust architecture - verify explicitly, use least privileged access, and assume breach - understanding why traditional perimeter-based security is insufficient for cloud environments.

diagrams.title: Cloud Security Incident Response Flowchart
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the structured process for handling security incidents in cloud environments from detection through recovery.
elements: Oval: Security Event Detection (SIEM alert, IDS trigger, user report)
Diamond: Triage and Analysis (Is this a real threat? What is the severity?)
Diamond: False Positive Decision (Yes/No paths)
Box: Containment - Isolation (quarantine affected VMs, revoke compromised credentials, block malicious IPs)
Box: Eradication - Remove Threat (malware removal, patch vulnerabilities, close attack vectors)
Box: Recovery - Restore Services (restore from clean backups, verify system integrity, resume operations)
Box: Post-Incident Review (document lessons learned, update playbooks, improve controls)
Arrow showing continuous improvement loop back to detection
Parallelogram: Communication (stakeholder notifications, compliance reporting)
Legend showing time estimates for each phase
Color coding: Red for detection/containment, Orange for analysis/eradication, Green for recovery
diagrams.purpose: Students will understand the structured incident response lifecycle specific to cloud environments, learning the proper sequence of actions and the importance of post-incident analysis for continuous security improvement.

diagrams.title: Cloud Native Security Services Architecture
diagrams.description: An architecture diagram showing how cloud-native security services integrate across AWS and Azure to provide comprehensive protection.
elements: Top layer: Security and Compliance Services - AWS Security Hub / Azure Security Center icons with dashboard indicators
Second layer: Identity Services - AWS IAM / Azure AD with MFA enforcement, conditional access
Third layer: Network Security - AWS WAF+Shield / Azure Firewall, NSGs, DDoS Protection
Fourth layer: Data Protection - AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault, encryption services, tokenization
Fifth layer: Threat Detection - AWS GuardDuty / Azure Defender, CloudTrail / Activity Log, SIEM integration
Bottom layer: Compute Security - AWS Inspector / Azure Security Center for containers, VM scanning
Horizontal data flow arrows between layers showing information sharing
API Gateway connecting services with dashed lines showing integration
Shared responsibility boundary line across the architecture
diagrams.purpose: Students will compare cloud-native security offerings from major providers, understanding how different services work together to provide layered security and which services address specific security requirements.

charts.title: Cloud Provider Security Services Comparison: AWS vs Azure
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Security Service Category, AWS Service, Azure Service, Key Features, Use Case. Rows: Identity & Access Management (IAM vs Azure AD, MFA, RBAC), Network Security (Security Groups/Network ACLs vs NSGs, DDoS Protection), Data Encryption (KMS, CloudHSM vs Key Vault, BitLocker), Threat Detection (GuardDuty, Inspector vs Defender for Cloud, Sentinel), Application Security (WAF, Shield vs Application Gateway WAF, DDoS Protection), Logging & Monitoring (CloudWatch, Config vs Monitor, Log Analytics). Each row includes feature checkmarks for capabilities like encryption at rest, encryption in transit, key management, compliance certifications.

charts.title: Classification of Cloud Security Controls
charts.content: Three-column classification: Preventive Controls (M IAM, encryption, firewalls, security groups, WAF, antivirus, patching, training), Detective Controls (SIEM, IDS/IPS, anomaly detection, log analysis, vulnerability scanning, CloudTrail monitoring, behavior analytics), Corrective Controls (incident response plans, backup/restore, failover, auto-scaling for DDoS, quarantine, isolation). Each category includes 5-6 specific cloud examples with icons representing each control type.

charts.title: Data Encryption Decision Flowchart
charts.content: Vertical flowchart: Start (Do you need to encrypt data?) -> Decision: Is data sensitive? (Yes/No) -> No path: Standard protection (transport security) -> Yes path -> Decision: Who manages keys? (Provider-managed vs Customer-managed) -> Provider-managed: Use service encryption (SSE, managed keys) -> Customer-managed: Use CMK (KMS, Cloud KMS) -> Decision: Need key rotation? (Yes/No) -> Yes: Configure automatic rotation policy -> Both paths converge to: Implement access policies -> Decision: Need field-level encryption? (Yes/No) -> Yes: Apply client-side encryption -> End: Monitor and audit encryption status. Color-coded decision diamonds and action boxes.

charts.title: Traditional Security vs Cloud Security Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with rows: Security Perimeter (network boundary vs identity boundary), Resource Management (static, known assets vs dynamic, ephemeral resources), Access Model (castle-and-moat vs Zero Trust), Responsibility (single organization vs shared responsibility), Visibility (on-premise logs vs distributed logging), Compliance (physical controls vs configuration controls), Incident Response (known infrastructure vs auto-scaling environments). Two-column format showing traditional IT approach versus cloud-native approach with key differences highlighted.

branches: Defense-in-Depth (Physical, Network, IAM, Application, Data, Endpoint)
Identity & Access Management (MFA, RBAC, Least Privilege, JIT Access, Password Policies)
Network Security (VPC, Security Groups, NACLs, WAF, DDoS Protection, VPN/Direct Connect)
Data Protection (Encryption at Rest/In Transit, Tokenization, DLP, Key Management, Backup/Recovery)
Security Monitoring (SIEM, Log Aggregation, IDS/IPS, Anomaly Detection, Alerting, Forensics)
Incident Response (Detection, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, Post-Incident Review)
Zero Trust Architecture (Never Trust, Always Verify, Microsegmentation, Continuous Validation)
DevSecOps (Shift-Left, Automated Security, CI/CD Security, Container Scanning)
Cloud-Native Security (Native Tools, Auto-Scaling Security, Serverless Security, Compliance Frameworks)
Shared Responsibility (Provider vs Customer Duties based on IaaS/PaaS/SaaS)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides a comprehensive overview connecting all key concepts from the module, helping students see how defense strategies interrelate and build upon each other to create a comprehensive cloud security posture.
